text: BERRY, or BERRI, an ancient prov. of France, which was divided into Haut B. and Bas B. Area 1,433,686 hectares; of which 691,547 hect. are now comprised in the dep. of Cher; 25,000 in that of Creuse; 22,750 in Loiret; 636,539 in L'Indre; 30,250 in La Vienne; and 27,600 in La Haute-Vienne. It had the title of a duchy, of which Boruges was the cap.; and the inhabitants were called Berrichons, or Berruyers. Its principal towns were Sancerre, Henrichemont, Vierzon, Mehun, Dun-le-Roi, Issoudun, Charost, Saint-Aignan, Chateauroux, Le Blanc, Argenton, and La Châtre. This territory took its name from the Bituriges of Cæsar. It was wrested from the Visigoths by Clovis. Eudes Arpin sold it to Philip I. in 1100; and from that period it became an appanage of the Crown. The last prince who held the title of duke de B. was the younger son of Charles X., who was assassinated in Feb. 1820.
BERRY-AU-BAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Neufchatel, on the Aisne. Pop. 484.
BERRY-HEAD, the S point of Torbay, on the coast of Devonshire, in N lat. 50° 24'.
BERRY ISLES, a group of small kays lying NW of New Providence, on the Great Bahama bank, in about N lat. 25° 40', W long. 77° 50'.
BERRYNARBOR, a parish in Devonshire, 3 m. ESE of Ilfracombe. Area 5,050 acres. Pop. 899.

BERRY-POMEROY, a parish in Devonshire, 2 m. ENE of Totness. Area 4,610 acres. Pop., with the township of Bridgetown, 1,149. The ruins of B. castle, founded here by Ralph de Pomeroy, in the 12th cent., are very picturesque.
BERRYSBURG, a village in Dauphin co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 39 m. N of Harrisburg.
BERRYSVILLE, a village in Clark co., Virginia, U. S., 158 m. from Richmond.
BERSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute Vienne, cant. of Bessines. Pop. 1,755.
BERSE'E, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Pont-a-Marcq. Pop. 1,615.

BERSENA, a town of Abyssinia, which rises in the country of the Abichu Gallas, and, in the lower part of its course, towards the Jamma, divides the prov. of Ensarro on the S, from that of Morat on the <math>N.-Beke.</math>
BERSENBRUCK, a village of Hanover, in the landrost of Osnabruck, on the Hase. Pop. 120.
BERSETZ, a town of Austria, in Illyria, in the circle of Pisino, on the gulf of Quarnero. It has some commerce in red wines, oil, honey, chestnuts, and fruit.
BERSHAM, a township in Denbighshire, 1 m. W of Wrexham. Pop. 1,716, a large proportion of whom are employed in iron-works in the vicinity.
BERSHI, a town of Central Africa, in the Houssa territory, to the W of Cashna, in N lat. 12° 50'.
BERSILLIES-L'ABBAYE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 22 m. SW of Charleroi. Pop. 287. An active smuggling trade with France is carried on here.
BERSON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. and 2 m. SE of Blaye. Pop. 1,923.
BERSTED, a parish in Sussex, 6 m. SE of Chichester. Area 2,750 acres. Pop. 2,490, of whom 194 were in the tything of North B.
BERT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Jaligny. Pop. 767. Coal is wrought here. BER
BERTHAMBOIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Lorquin. Pop. 1,263.
BERTHELE'VILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant, of Gondrecourt, on the Ornain. Pop. 90.

BERTHELSDORF, a village of Saxony, in the circle of Budessin, W of Zittau. Pop. 1,500. It is the seat of the central consistory of the Moravian brethren.—Also a town of Prussia, in Silesia, W of Hirschberg. Pop. 600. The counts of Sandreczky have a castle here.
BERTHENICOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Moy, 8 m. from Saint- Quentin. Pop. 300. Linen thread is made here.
BERTHENOUX (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre, cant. of La Châtre. Pop. 1,427.
BERTHEVIN-LA-TANNIE'RE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Landivy. Pop. 939.
BERTHEVIN-SUR-VICOIN (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Laval. Pop. 1,984.

BERTHIER, a county of Lower Canada, in the district of Montreal; bounded on the NE by the co. of St. Maurice; on the E and SE by the St. Lawrence; on the SW by the co. of Lachenaie; and on the NW by the northern boundary of the prov. It is 240 m. in length, and 24 m. broad. Area 5,760 sq. m. Pop. in 1836, 17,695. The surface for a distance of 15 m. from the St. Lawrence is generally level. It is watered by the Bayonne, Chicot, Great and Little Chaloupe, St. Charles, St. Joseph, St. John, and Brook rivers.—The v. of B. is situated on the Chenail du Nord, on the main road to Quebec, midway between Montreal and Three rivers. Pop. 850.
BERTHOLDS (GROSS), a town of Lower Austria, in the gov. of Vienna, circle of Krems, 12 m. SW of Weitra. Pop. 809. There are glass and crystal manufactories here.
BERTHOLONE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Laissac. Pop. 1,452.
BERTHOLSDORF, or Petersdorf, a town of Lower Austria, 9 m. SW of Vienna. Pop. 1,821.
BERTHOLSHOFEN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, SE of Kaufbeuern. Pop. 307.
BERTHOUD. See BURGDORF.

BERTIE, a county in the NE part of the state of Carolina, U. S. It is bounded on the S by the Roanoke river. Area 1,000 sq. m. The surface is generally level, and in some places marshy. Its chief productions are cotton and tobacco. Pop. 12,175. Capital, Windsor. — Also a township of Upper Canada, bounded on the E and NE by the Niagara river, and on the S by Lake Erie. Pop. 2,318, chiefly of Pennsylvanian Dutch descent. The village of Waterloo and Fort Erie are in this township.
BERTIGNAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Saint-Amand. Pop. 2,690.
BERTINCOURT (OSSIMONT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Arras.—The cant. comprises 17 com.—The com. is 19 m. SSE of Arras. Pop. 1,416.
BERTINORO, a town in the States-of-the-Church, 7 m. SE of Forli, on the Ronco. Pop. 4,000. It is the see of the bishop of B. et Sarsina. Excellent wines are produced in the vicinity.

BERTIOGA, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 18 m. ESE of Barbacena.—Also the name of a channel which separates the island of Santo-Amaro, or Guahiba, from the mainland in the Brazilian prov. of San Paulo.
BERTIOLO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 12 m. SW of Udine. Pop. 2,300.
BERTRAND, a township in Berrien co., in the state of Michigan, 186 m. W by S of Detroit, on the E bank of the St. Joseph river. Pop. 1,298.

BERTRAND-DE-COMINGES (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Haute- Garonne, arrond. of Saint-Gaudens.—The cant. comprises 23 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,854.—The com. is 8 m. SW of Saint-Gaudens, near the Garonne. Pop. 847. It possesses extensive marble quarries and works. The v. is supposed to occupy the site of the Roman Lugdunum Convenarum.
BERTREN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees, cant. of Mauléon. Pop. 305.
BERTRY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Clary. Pop. 1,602.
BERU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Tonnere. Pop. 336.
BERUM, a town of Hanover, in the landrost and 15 m. NW of Aurich. Pop. 855.
BERUN, a town of Prussia, in Silesia, in the gov. of Oppeln, 12 m. NE of Pless. Pop. 800.

BERVIE, a parish and burgh in Kincardineshire. Area of p. 2,000 acres. Pop. 1,342, of whom 864 were in the town of B., or INVERBERVIE, which is situated about a mile from the mouth of the B., on the post-road from Dundee to Aberdeen, 38 m. N of Edinburgh. It unites with the Montrose district of burghs in returning a member to parliament. The number of electors in 1842-3 was 39. The only manufacture in the place is that of linen-weaving. Grain and cattle markets are occasionally held.
BERVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Doudeville. Pop. 1,148.
BERWICK, a parish in Sussex, 7 m. SE of Lewis. Area 1,250 acres. Pop. 199.—Also a hamlet in Llanelly p., Carmarthenshire. Pop. 981.

BERWICK, a village in Columbia co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 97 m. N by E of Harrisburg, on the N side of the Susquehanna, over which there is here a bridge 1,260 ft. long.—Also a township in York co., Maine, 16 m. NW of Portsmouth, on the Salmon-fall river. Pop. 1,698.—Also a township in Adams co., Pennsylvania, 41 m. SW of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,462.
BERWICK-BASSETT, a parish in Wilts, in Calne hundred. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 175.

BERWICK (NORTH), a parish and burgh in Haddingtonshire, on the S coast of the frith of Forth. Pop. of the p. in 1801, 1,583; in 1841, 1,708.—The burgh and port is 22 m. NE by E of Edinburgh, and 8 m. NNE of Haddington. It has a small but useful harbour, of about three-quarters of an acre in extent, enclosed by piers. The entrance is 251 ft. wide, with a depth of 13 ft. at the pier-heads. It is not easy of access, but once entered, it can be secured from bad weather by boom-gates. The arrivals in cargo in 1846 were 65 vessels of 1,941 tons; the dues levied were £71. The locality, on the most projecting part of the coast of East Lothian, is well marked from a distance by the great leading objects, the Bass rock and North Berwick law; and it has been considered by some persons favourable for the site of a national harbour to shelter coasting-vessels waiting for a wind, or as a look-out station for war-steamers. B. is a place otherwise of small trade, and the few coasting-vessels belonging to its port are chiefly employed in the exportation of agricultural produce, and the importation of bone-dust and other manures for the farmers of the county. A branch railway, connecting the town with the North British line, is nearly completed. B. is a contributory burgh to the Haddington district in returning a member to parliament. Number of electors in 1842-3, 36.
BERWICK-ST.-JAMES, a parish in Wilts, 5½ m. WSW of Amesbury. Area 2,370 acres. Pop. 247.

BERWICK-ST.-JOHN, a parish in Wilts, 5½ and the control of the section of the control of the control of the control of the control of the control of the control of the control of the control of the control of the control of the control of the control of the cont BER m. E by S of Shaftesbury. Area 4,230 acres. Pop. 419.

BERWICK-ST.-LEONARD, a parish in Wilts, 1 m. E of Hindon. Area 970 acres. Pop. 41. · BERWICK-UPON-TWEED, a seaport town and parish, on the N bank of the Tweed, at its confluence with the sea, in the co. of Northumberland; 337 m. N by W of London; 54 m. ESE of Edinburgh; and 64 m. N by W of Newcastle-on-Tyne; in 55° 46′ 21" N lat., and 1° 59′ 41" W long. Area of the p., 5,120 acres. Pop. in 1841, 8,484; in 1801, 7,187. The town of B., equally celebrated in the annals of England and of Scotland, includes within its walls an area of little more than 11 m. in circumf.; and consists of streets which for the most part are narrow, straggling, and irregular. Its principal buildings are: the town-hall, the governor's house, the barracks, and the bridge, consisting of 15 noble arches, and measuring 1,164 ft. in length, though only 17 ft. wide. The houses are in general old. The town is fortified, but of the more ancient fortifications few traces remain. The modern works consist of an earthen rampart, substantially reveted, with bastions, flanks, and a ditch, on the N and E; and on the S and W, of high walls flanked with cannon, under which the river flows. The ramparts form an agreeable promenade. The suburb, called Castlegate, is situated without the walls, near the NW part of the town; and a long range of houses called the Greens, stretches from Castlegate eastward. Some manufactures are carried on; among which are those of linen, sack, and sail-cloth, ropes, hosiery, carpets, &c. There is also an iron-foundry. B. has an extensive coasting-commerce. Timber, iron, hemp, flax, bones, tallow, and blubber, form the principal imports; corn, coals, wool, salmon, pork, and eggs, are exported in considerable quantity. Salmon are caught in great numbers in the Tweed, and sent to London; and there is a considerable fishery of white fish and lobsters off the coast. About 800 of the inhabitants are connected with these fisheries. The salmon-fisheries produced a rental of £20,000 20 years ago; but do not now reach £4,000.—The port of B. is frequented only by small vessels,—a bar at the entrance of the harbour rendering it unfit for large ones. According to returns made in 1828, the number of vessels belonging to the port was 54; and the average burden 90 tons. In 1834, the tonnage inwards, coasting and foreign, amounted to 28,768 tons; outwards, 40,715. In 1845, the tonnage inwards, exclusive of steam-vessels, was 39,168 tons; outwards, 36,164. Of this tonnage about 4,000 was colonial and foreign. The steam tonnage in 1845 was about 18,000 tons inwards and outwards. The ships belonging to the port in 1846 were 76, of a total tonnage of 5,818 tons. The revenue of the harbour in 1841 was £2,846; in 1843, £2,967. For the improvement and protection of this harbour, a stone-pier, half-amile in length, has been constructed, and a lighthouse erected at its extremity. There are no docks. The Tweed is navigable as high as the bridge, and the tide flows about 7 m. farther up. The ordinary spring-tides rise 15 ft.—The North British and the Newcastle and Berwick railways unite at B. By the former B. is 58 m. distant from Edinburgh; and by the latter, 65 m. from Newcastle. These railways are connected and carried across the Tweed, a few yards above the town of B., by a gigantic viaduct, consisting of 28 semicircular arches, each 611 ft. span, resting on lofty piers, carrying the level of the railway 103 ft. above high water mark, 126 ft. above low water mark, and 137 ft. above the deepest part of the bed of the river. The whole length of the bridge, with abutments and wingwalls, is 2,140 ft. The 28 arches are divided into two series by a broad pier, 28 ft. in thickness in the middle. The piers of the arches are 81 ft. in thickness at the springing, increasing by steps towards the bottom. A railway is now executing between Berwick and Kelso. The charter, under which B. is now incorporated, was granted by James VI. By the municipal act of 1835, the borough has been divided into three wards, to each of which six councillors are apportioned. A commission of the peace and court of quarter-sessions have been recently granted. In all the courts, civil and criminal, the proceedings are regulated by English law: although it is difficult to determine, either historically or topographically, whether B. belongs to England or Scotland. The annual value of the corporation property in 1837 was £12,754, of which £3,849 arose out of rents; the expenditure in that year was £12,369; the debt is about £60,000.—B. has returned two members to parliament since the reign of Philip and Mary; and has always been what is called an open borough, though much influenced by the number of residents in the pay of government. The right of election was vested in all the burgesses, whether resident or not, in number about 1,000. Under the reform act, the p. of Berwick, and the townships of Tweedmouth and Spittal, on the S side of the river, unite in returning two members to parliament. The pop. of the united parliamentary burgh in 1841 was 12,578. Number of electors in 1839-40, 755; in 1842-3, 769. The liberties of Berwick extend from the entrance of the harbour northwards to the grounds of Lamberton, whence they are bounded by a line crossing the river Whitadder, and ending at the Tweed; the other boundary line runs down close by the river to its termination at the bar, there forming an equilateral angle, two sides of which are 2 m. each, and the other side somewhat more. Within these liberties—which form one parish—are a number of private estates; a part is let out upon leases; and the rest, measuring about 4,500 acres, is parcelled out into separate allotments called "Burgess' meadows," or stints, which are occupied by the oldest burgesses or their widows, and are worth from £5 to £15 per annum. The sixth pillar upon Berwick bridge separates Berwick from the co.-palatine of Durham. The name of Berwick is supposed by Leland to have been originally Aberwick, from the British words aber, 'the mouth of a river,' and wic, 'a town.' Camden and some other antiquaries derive it from the ancient word berewic, which is used in old records to designate a hamlet attached to a place of greater importance. The supposition of Leland seems to be the better of the two; but Chalmers suggests that the name may have originated in the Anglo-Saxon bar, i. e. 'bare,' and wic, i. e. vicus, castellum, sinus, 'the curving-reach of a river.' B. has been frequently besieged, and in the wars between the two kingdoms frequently changed masters, till 1482, when it finally fell into the hands of Edward IV. By treaty of Edward VI., and Mary, queen of Scots, it was erected into an independent town; Elizabeth, however, resumed possession of it, and strengthened the fortifications. On the accession of James VI to the throne of England, its importance as a frontier-town was extinguished; and as a fortified town it has only once appeared in history since that period, having been garrisoned by the parliament in the civil war in the reign of Charles L.

BERWICKSHIRE, a county forming the SE extremity of Scotland, and stretching along the NE border of England. Its principal division was anciently called the Merse, or March, a name which it still retains, and which probably signifies 'the border-district,' or 'frontier-province.' This denomination is still often used, loosely, for the whole county. The modern name, Berwickshire, is derived from the town of Berwick-upon-Tweed,-once its chief burgh or county-town; but which, after the demise of Elizabeth, and the accession of James VI. of Scotland to the English throne, was constituted a peculiar jurisdiction, hypothetically separate from both kingdoms, and virtually forming a distinct county. Extent and Area.] Mr. Blackadder estimates the extreme length of the co., from E to W, at 311 m., and its extreme breadth, from N to S, at 193 m.; the mean length at 261 m., and the mean breadth at 17 m.; and the total contents at 285,440 acres. But, Mr. Kerr says, "from a very careful consideration of the map itself, attentively measured by its own scale, the mean length appears to be 28 m., the mean breadth 17 m., and the consequent contents 304,640 acres." Coasts, &c.] B. is bounded on the E by the German ocean; along which, from the boundaries of Berwick township to St. Abb's-head, its coast trends NNW for 85 m. It then takes a WNW direction, for other 9 m., to its point of junction with East Lothian at Dunglass bridge. By the revenue laws, this latter part of the coast is considered as being within the limits of the frith of Forth. Almost the whole of this coast consists of bold rocky precipices of considerable altitude; and is nearly inaccessible, except at Eyemouth and Coldingham bays, and two or three other places accessible to fishing-boats. The whole irregular northern boundary skirts with East Lothian, along the mountain-range of Lammermoor; while the most northerly part of the co. is situated beyond, or to the N of the Lammermoor hills, and is continuous with the extensive and fertile vale of the Lothians. Clint-hill, one of the highest of the Lammermoor chain, in the p. of Channelkirk, at the NW extremity of the county, rises 1,544 ft. above sea-level. Lammerlaw, in the p. of Lauder, has an alt. of 1,500 ft. The general range of these mountains declines as it approaches the sea, averaging about 1,000 ft. in perpendicular elevation; and it terminates in three precipitous promontories, at Fast-castle, Ernscleugh, and St. Abb's-head; which last is detached from the extremity of the chain by a deep narrow dell almost level with high water mark at spring-tides. The western irregular limit of Berwickshire is partly with Mid-Lothian, towards the N, but chiefly with Roxburghshire, from which it is partly divided, on that side, by the lower and principal stream of the Leader water, to its junction with the river Tweed near Melrose. Excepting a portion of Roxburghshire adjoining Kelso, and the township of Berwick, both of which are on the N side of the Tweed, that beautiful river, in a meandering course of about 40 m., forms the southern boundary of this co., dividing it from Roxburghshire on the W, Northumberland in the middle, and North Durham on the E, of this line of division. North Durham is a detached portion of the co.-palatine of Durham, having the whole extent of Northumberland interposed between it and the main body of the patrimony of St. Cuthbert, which once held extensive possessions in Scotland also. From Berwick township, Berwickshire is divided by a semilunar dry march. Rivers.] Excepting the Eye, with its scanty tributaries, which falls into Eyemouth bay, and a small number of inconsiderable brooks which run separately into the sea, all the streams of Berwickshire contribute to swell the waters of the Tweed. The other streams in the county are usually denominated waters,—a kind of intermediate provincial term, between the dignity of a river, and the insignificancy of a brook, which latter is called a burn in Scotland. Still smaller rills, especially in marshy places, are often called sykes,-The Leader, with its numerous burns, winds through the vale of Lauderdale.—The Whitadder and Blackadder—quasi White and Black waters, owing to their respective tinges when in flood—are next to exclusively Berwickshire streams. Dye, one of the main sources of Whitadder, rises by several brooks or feeders, on the ridge of hills which separate Lauderdale from Lammermoor. The Whitadder proper, rising within East Lothian, at an elevation of 1,150 ft., unites with Dye in a romantic vale of some extent, in the bosom of the Lammermoor hills; and having received the Blackadder much lower down, at Allanton, in the vale of the Merse, unites with the Tweed within Berwick bounds, about 3 miles from the sea.—The Blackadder and its streamlets, or feeders, rise from the southern slopes of the Lammermoor and Lauderdale hills, at an elevation of about 1,130 ft.; and, after winding through the vale of the Merse, joins the Whitadder between Allanbank and Ninewells.—The small stream of the Eden principally belongs to that portion of Roxburghshire which indents into this county, on the N side of Tweed, into which that small river flows a few miles below Kelso.—The Leet, another small stream, belongs entirely to the "how of the Merse," and joins Tweed at Coldstream.—The small river Eye, with a few feeders-particularly the Ale and Hornwaters a narrow but fertile vale in the east end of the Merse; several of its upper streamlets wind among some narrow valleys towards the west end of the Lammermoor hills. Its peculiar source is within East Lothian. At one place,—from near Ayton to near Chirnside, -a narrow winding vale, of very inconsiderable elevation, almost permits the Whitadder and Eye to unite.-Midway between, Billy bog or Billy mire discharges its superfluous waters into both rivers,—eastwards, by the Horn burn, into Eye, with just sufficient declivity for its ready passage; westwards, by the Billy burn, into Whitadder. This singular vale is about 5 m. long, and has a northern branch, more elevated. The Ale, Wedderburn, and many other brooks, are too inconsiderable to require any special notice. All the rivers, waters, and brooks in this county abound with trout of different kinds; some contain a few pike and perch, and all have plenty of eels. There are no lakes of any importance in the county. Dunse spa, once in some little repute as a mineral spring, has fallen into complete neglect. Soil.] This county possesses every variety of soil, from the most stubborn clay to the most barren sand or gravel, but none whatever of a chalky or calcareous nature. Along the banks of Tweed, Whitadder, and Blackadder, there is an extensive tract of fine deep free loam, often upon a gravel bottom, parts of the co., marshy spots are found in the hollows, into which the water of springs or small rills are poured from the adjoining slopes. These are overgrown with rushes or other marsh-plants, and are inundated with water in rainy weather. Dogden moss, near Polwart, covers about 500 acres, and is in some places 10 ft. deep. Climate.] In spring the prevalent winds are from the E points, and are attended by cold raw weather and frequent frosts. This ungenial temperature is apt to continue far into the summer, probably owing to the neighbourhood of the ocean; but, from the same cause, the winters are seldom of long continuance, or peculiar severity. This influence, however, lessens perceptibly in all respects at 8 or 10 m. from the sea; and the winters in the Lammermoor hills and in Lauderdale are severe and continued, though not more so than in the hills of Northumberland, or of Yorkshire. In autumn the prevailing winds are from the W. Minerals.] Several endeavours have been made to discover a workable seam of coal in Berwickshire. In the estate of Lamberton, contiguous to Berwick bounds, at the SE extremity of the co., a stratum of coal has long been known, which crops out on the sea-banks near the fishing-hamlet of Ross. Some PARAMANANANANANANANANANANANANANANANANANAN coal has also been found in the parishes of Mordington and Cockburnspath. An attempt was once made to dig copper ore, at Ordwell on the Whitadder; but it has been long abandoned. More recent attempts to work this mineral at St. Bathens, and also near the old church of Ellim, in Longformacus parish, have proved equally abortive. No indications of lead, tin, antimony, or any other metallic ore-copper and perhaps red iron-ore excepted-are known to exist in this county. Some slight trials were made many years ago of a ferruginous claystone rock, on the estate of Ayton, as an iron-stone or ore of iron; but it was found too poor in metal to defray the expense of transport to Carron iron-works. In some inland parts of the county there are a few veins of limestone; but hitherto, the county has been mainly dependent on its neighbours for the two great articles of domestic comfort and agricultural improvement, coal and lime. In many parts of the county, shell marl has been found in small quantities. Trap whinstone, and amorphous basalt, interspersed with irregularly stratified clay-stone, are almost universal. In several places, rocks of breccia, or coarse pudding-stone, are found. Agriculture, &c.] For the purposes of agricultural inquiry, the whole co. may be conveniently considered under two districts,-the Merse and Lammermoor; the former including all the comparatively low land along Tweed, Whitadder, Blackadder, and Eye; and the latter comprehending Lauderdale, along with the more eastern hilly country peculiarly called Lammermoor. The Merse forms the largest piece of compact level ground-diversified only by a few gentle undulations—to be found in Scotland. Mr. Blackadder estimates this division to contain 100,226 acres. The remainder of the county-with a small exception—is therefore to be considered as forming the Lammermoor district; and, according to the same authority, should contain 185,214 acres. But Mr. B. computes that there are 7,280 acres of lowland and arable slopes of the lower hills in Lauderdale, besides a detached portion of lowland containing 2,200 acres, at the NE corner of the county, in the p. of Cockburnspath. The whole may be estimated and distributed as in the following table: Acres. Or, leaving out Berwick township, . . . 285,440 Manufactures, &c.] B. is a strictly pastoral and agricultural district. The only manufacture of any importance, within the county, is that of paper. Three mills in 1808 gave employment to 200 individuals, and paid above £4,000 yearly of excise duties. The manufacture of woollens and linens is confined, in the former, entirely to coarse goods for ordinary use; and, in the latter, to household linens for farmers and labourers' families. The manufacture of ginghams has been introduced with great success at Earlston. The fishery upon the coast is not of very material importance. Fish-carriers, called cadgers, purchase from the fishers, and distribute the white fish, codlings, haddock, whitings, skate, halybut, and flounders, and a few turbot, into the inland country; but the greater proportion of the fish now caught is sent by railway to Edinburgh and to the south. The herring fishery on the coast is exceedingly precarious; but during some seasons no less than 10,000 barrels have been brought into Eyemouth. A few red herring-houses at Eyemouth were once well-employed. Some boats or small vessels go annually to the herring-fishery on the coast of Caithness. The salmon fishery in Tweed is of considerable importance, but the principal share of it belongs to the township of Berwick, and the opposite side of the Tweed. The chief line of road running through this county is the great post road from Berwick to Edinburgh, which follows the outline of the coast, generally at a little distance from the sea. The North British railway, which has nearly the same course, with one extensive deviation, is now the principal line of traffic and transit. Population.] The pop. of the co. was in 1801, 30,621; in 1811, 30,779; in 1821, 33,385; in 1831, 34,048; in 1841, 34,438. In 1821, the number of houses inhabited was 5,803; in 1831 it was 6,159; in 1841, 7,408. The number of families in 1821 was 7,165; in 1831 it was 7,385. The number of families employed in agriculture in 1821 was 3,334; in 1831, 2,921. The number of families employed in trade, manufactures, and handicraft in 1821 was 1,923; in 1831, 1,915. The number of all other families not comprised in the two preceding classes in 1821 was 1,908; in 1831, 2,549.—The number of paupers in 1845-6 was 1,000: the sum expended on them £7,009. Divisions, Towns, &c.] Since the dismemberment of Berwick from Scotland, Lauder remains the only royal burgh in the co.; and in conjunction with Jedburgh, Haddington, Dunbar, and North Berwick, sends one representative to parliament. Greenlaw, a small village 37 m. SE of Edinburgh, in an inconvenient situation for the purpose, is the county-town. Dunse and Coldstream are neither of them of much importance; but Dunse seems better fitted—from being more central to the chief population—than Greenlaw for being the county town. Eyemouth, little better than a fishing village, is the only seaport within the county. B. is divided into 33 parishes, of which 20 may be considered as in the Merse, and 13 in Lammermoor. The largest parishes are those of Lauder and Coldingham. The number of parochial schools within the county in 1834 was 34; and of private schools, 59. The total number of scholars was estimated at 4,998. History.] At the period of the Roman invasion, B. was inhabited by the Ottadini. It was afterwards invaded by bands of Saxons from Germany, who ingrafted their language and manners on those of the original inhabitants. The whole area of B. was comprehended in the Saxon territory, which received the name of Saxonia in the Scoto-Irish Chronicle, but was called Bernicia in the age of Bede. Until 1020, this district was included within the kingdom of Northumberland. In that year it was ceded to Malcolm II. by Cospatrick, Earl of Northumberland, who, settling in Scotland, was created Earl of Dunbar. In 1097 Edgar, the son of Malcolm, acquired the sovereignty of Berwickshire, which on his death he bequeathed, along with part of Cumberland and Lothian, to his brother David. Under this personage B. rose into consequence, and the town of Berwick came to be a seat of merchandise, and known for the value of its fisheries. About this epoch many Norman and Anglo- Saxon families settled in Berwickshire, and laid the foundation of a number of noble houses still ranked in the peerage of the country. It appears likewise that the town of B. became a settlement of Flemish and other foreign tradesmen. B. suffered in the succeeding centuries in all the wars between the two hostile nations, and was occasionally involved in disputes with its neighbour the palatine bishop of Durham. The town of B., and its bridge across the Tweed, were in general chief and special objects of dispute between the belligerents.
BERWILLER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Soultz. Pop. 1,012.
BERWINNE, a river of Belgium, in the dep. of Liege, flowing into the Meuse, on the r. bank, 2 m. above Vise, after a course, in a WNW and N by W direction, of about 20 m.

BERWYN, a mountain-range of N Wales, about 16 m. in length, traversing Merionethshire from NE to SW. Its principal summits are Cader-Berwyn, BES alt. 2,563 ft.; Arran-Fowddy, 2,955 ft.; and Cader- Idris, 2,914 ft.
BERZA-PALANKA, a fortified village of Turkey, in Servia, in the sanjak of Semendria, on the r. bank of the Danube, 12 m. SW of Chernetz.
BERZENCZE, or Bressnitz, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Sumegh, march of Martzali, 32 m. WNW of Szigetár, in N lat. 46° 12' 34". Pop. 2,095. The counts of Festetits have a chateau here.
BERZESI, or Bersezzi, an islet in the gulf of Genoa, 3 m. NNE from Cape Noli, separated from the main by a channel about 350 fath. in breadth.— Also a village on the main, 1 m. NNE of this islet.
BERZEVICZE, a village of Hungary, in the com. of Sarosch, between Zeben and Bartfeld, on a headstream of the Tarcza.
BERZOSA, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. W of Soria, on the l. bank of the Rio de Rejas. -Also a v. in the prov. and 20 m. NE of Burgos, on the l. bank of the Matapan.
BESALU, a small town of Spain, in the prov. and 12 m. NNW of Gerona, on the Fluvia river.

BESANÇON, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and fortified city of France, in the dep. of Doubs.—The arrond. has an area of 138,074 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 99,025; in 1846, 109,136. It comprises the 7 cant. of Amancey, Audeux, B., Boussières, Marchaux, Ornans, and Quingey.-The cant. comprises 14 com., and had a pop. of 34,011 in 1831. -The town, which is the cap. of the dep., is seated on the Doubs river, between two hills, in N lat. 47° 13' 46", E long. 6° 1' 56", 56 m. E of Dijon; 240 m. SSE of Paris. The alt. of the river at this point is 236 metres, or 774 ft. above sea-level; that of the citadel of B. 392 metres, or 1,286 ft. The river Doubs divides the city into two parts, connected by an ancient and narrow bridge, and nearly insulates the larger and older portion of the city. The citadel occupies the isthmus of the peninsula on which the city stands; and the city is further defended by a wall, flanked with eight towers, and having six gates. The Porte Taillée is of solid masonry, and was originally constructed by the Romans to give entrance to an aqueduct by which B. was supplied with water from the village of Arcier, 7 m. distant. The lower town consists chiefly of three fine streets. The cathedral of St. Jean, the Palais de justice, the hospital of St. Jacques, and the college, are the principal edifices, but present nothing very remarkable. Numerous architectural remains of Roman times are still traceable.—B. is on the line of inland navigation by which the Rhine and the Rhone are linked together, and is the entrepot of an extensive commerce, especially with Switzerland, of which the principal articles are iron, cattle, horses, butter, salt, cheese, com, wine, muslins, woollens, leather, and skins. The octroi dues in 1833 amounted to 210,789 francs.— The principal manufactures are clocks and watches, military arms, jewellery, linen and woollen stuffs, leather, printing types, wines, seltzer water, and vinegar.—The pop. of B., in 1789, was 20,228; in 1836 it was 29,718; in 1846, 33,788.—It is the seat of an archb., whose suffragans are the bishops of Strasburg, Metz, Verdun, Belley, Saint-Die, and Nancy, and whose dio. comprises the dep. of Doubs and of Haute- Saone. It is also the seat of a Protestant consistory, a cour royale, a tribunal of commerce, a university academy, a secondary school of medicine, a royal college, a normal school, and a theological seminary. It has a public library containing 60,000 vols., a museum, and a botanical garden. - So early as the period of the invasion of Gaul by the Romans, B. was a town of some consequence, and received successively the names of Oppidum Sequanorum, Chrysopolis, Bisantium, and Visontium or Vesontio, of which the present name is a corruption. Cæsar took it from the Sequani, and made it the basis of his campaign in this quarter of Gaul. It was at the height of its prosperity in the reign of Aurelian; but was nearly destroyed, first by the Alemanni, and afterwards by the Huns, and was not rebuilt till the time of the Burgundians; after which it received great privileges as a city of the empire, but was ceded to Spain at the peace of Westphalia. In 1660 and 1674, it was taken by the French; and was confirmed to that power, with the rest of the province, at the peace of Nimeguen. B. was before the revolution the seat of a governor-general, a parliament, an intendant, and various provincial offices. The archb. was a prince of the empire until the peace of Ryswick brought him under the dominion of France. The university, which was dissolved at the revolution, was re-established, in the form of a lyceum and college, in 1801. B. was fruitlessly invested by the allies in 1814.—It was the birth-place of the Jesuit Nonotte, the antagonist of Voltaire, the minister Courvoisier, the prince of Montbarrey, Marshal Moncey, the academicians Suard and Droy, of Ch. Nodier, and Victor Hugo.- Charnage, Histoire de la ville et dio. de B., 2 vols. 4to., 1750. — Delacroix, Recherches archeologiques sur les monuments de B. 1842.—Almanack historique de B. 1785-6.

BESATE, a town of Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, in the deleg. and 12 m. NW of Pavia, between the canal of Bereguardo and the l. bank of the Ticino. Pop. 1,800. Rice is extensively cultivated in the environs.
BESAVA, a river of Turkey, in Servia, in the sanjak of Semendria, flowing into the Morava on the r. bank.
BESAZIO, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Ticino, district of Mendrisio. Pop. 220. Fine broccatelle marble is quarried in the environs.
BESBOROUGH, an island off the coast of Russian America, in Norton sound, in N lat. 64°.

BESCA, a village of Illyria, in the circle of Fiume, on the SE coast of the island of Veglia, at the lower end of a fine fertile valley 7 m. in length, which stretches towards the W, and produces wine of a fine quality. It forms a place of autumnal resort to the citizens of Veglia.—Fortis.

BESCARA, or BISKARA, a town of Algiers, in the subdivision of Bathna, in N lat. 34°, 210 m. SE of Algiers, on the lower slope of Mount Auress. The French have a small garrison here which keeps the neighbouring villages of the Zibans in check. The native pop. is about 2,500. The market is supplied with dates, henna, red pepper, woollen stuffs from Suf and Tuggurt, and some merchandise from the Sahara. The only manufactures are those of haicks, burnouses, pottery, and bricks. The environs are irrigated by the Zeyur, which descends from the Auress, and after a winding course of about 40 m., disappears in the sands of the wadi Adjedid. Palms and olives are cultivated by means of the water supplied by this stream. At the foot of a range of hills a little to the W of B. is a hot salt spring called Ain-Enchichi. Towards the S the country becomes flat and sandy.
BESEL, a town of Belgium, in Limburg, SW of Venloo. Pop. 1,084.
BESENSTADT, a town of Prussia, in Saxony, in the circle of Mannsfeld.

BESED, a town of Russia, which rises in the district of Roslool, in the gov. of Smolensk; divides that gov. from Mohilev in the early part of its course; enters the gov. of Chernigov, and then, re-entering Mohilev, falls into the Soj, 14 m. N of Novo-Bielitza, after a course of 90 m. from NE to SW.
BESERG HENLU, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Caramania, 24 m. N of Akserai. I. BES
BESFORD, a township in the p. of Shawbury, in the co. of Salop. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. 167.- Also a chapelry in Worcestershire, 2 m. W by S of Pershore. Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 179.
BESGAS (Las), a town of Spain, in the prov. and 18 m. NE of Burgos, on the r. bank of the Oca.

BESHEK, a lake of Turkey in Macedonia, on the r. of the road from Salonica to Klissala. Clarke, who saw it in the early part of January, estimated its length at about 12 m. from E to W, and its breadth at from 6 m. to 8 m.; and says that the plain in which it lies may be regarded as a continuation of that wherein the smaller lake of St. Basil is situated. It abounds in fish, of which Dr. Sibthorpe took 11 different species. There are two villages, distinguished as Mirsa B., and Trana B., or the Great B. and Little B., near this lake and within 2 miles of each other. Clarke inclines to identify this lake with the Bolbæan lake of the ancients. It is called Robios by the Greeks. -Clarke's Travels, vol. viii. - Walpole's Memoirs relating to Turkey.

BESHIRI, or BESHIRAI, a Maronite village of Syria, in the pash. of Tripoli, in a rich and picturesque valley watered by the Kadesha, on the ascent of Lebanon, on the road from the v. of Eden to the cedar-grove of Lebanon. Burckhardt says it consists of about 120 houses. It is surrounded by groves of mulberries and fruit trees, and fields or rather terraces of dhourra and corn.

BESHKOLSKOI, a long saline lake, or rather a series of saline lakes and pools and sedgy marshes, in the Russian gov. of Astrakan, on the road from Astrakan to Glubokinskoi. These lakes appear to have originated in an inlet of the Caspian sea, which has become gradually choked by drift-sand.—Pallas.

BESHTAU, a calcareous ridge of mountains in S Russia, forming the most northern spur of the Caucasus. It runs between the valleys of the Kuma and the Kuban; and rises progressively towards the SW. On the N and E an extensive plain presents itself. Its principal summit—which is generally known as the B. par excellence—appears to rise from a kind of table-land greatly elevated above the level of the Kuma and the Podkuma. It is a mass of ancient limestone, presenting few fossils. From near its summit several narrow ridges run off towards the four adjacent mountains; whence the name Beshtau, or 'the Five Mounts.' Pallas gives a full notice of the botany of this region.

BESIGHEIM, a small town of Wurtemberg, in the Neckar circle, at the influx of the Enz into the Neckar, 18 m. N of Stutgard. Pop. 2,412. Alt. above sea-level 600 ft. It has two old castles called Roman towers, and is the head of an upper bailiwick.
BESIKTASH, a village of European Turkey, on the Black sea, near the Tophana suburb of Constantinople. The famous Hayradin Barbarossa lies interred here, in a mosque of his own founding.

BESITUN, a village or caravanserai, in the prov. of Irak, between Sahana and Kermanshaw, 16 m. from the former, and 20 m. from the latter place. Near this place are some curiously sculptured rocks, which are described by Kinneir, in his Geog. Memoir.

BESLINIANS, or BESSLINIZI, a tribe in the western part of Circassia, who, according to a recent Russian account, numbered 17,000 families in 1804. They reside beyond the Kuban, on the Upper Laba, at its exit from the high mountains, and extend themselves as far as the Khots, and to the source of the Psefir. where it touches on the Moukhoch or Mashokhi. The chiefs of this tribe are the ancestors of those of the Kabardali. The Beslinians join with the Kabardinians and Nogays, established on the Russian territory, in their forays. They are rich in cattle, especially in sheep. In winter they keep their cattle near their dwellings on the Laba, in enclosures of hurdles. In spring and autumn they graze them on the banks of the Urup, the Great Injek, and the salt lake of Kasma.
BESLON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Perey. Pop. 1,051.
BESNE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Pont-Chateau. Pop. 1,083.
BESNEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Saint-Sauveur-sur-Douve. Pop. 1,487.
BESOS, a river of Spain, in Catalonia, which rises near Vilamajor, runs from NE to SW, and falls into the Mediterranean near Barcelona, after a short course.

BESRODNAYA SLOBODA, or VERKNEI AKHTUBINSKOI GORODOK, a village of Asiatic Russia, between the rivers Volga and Akhtuba, in the vicinity of the Caspian. The empress Elizabeth established a settlement here for the culture of silk, and sent Armenians and other foreigners to assist its progress. Catherine II., following up the designs of her predecessor, prepared instructions under her own hand to promote this object, and 1,300 families were brought to the banks of the Akhtuba, where they were located in six villages, with certain immunities. Though these exertions were attended with partial success, the cultivators testified an invincible dislike to their occupation, and the quantity of silk declined yearly under their reluctant labours: at last they contrived secretly to destroy the worms by sprinkling them with salt, and to set fire to the grass around the mulberry-trees. The government in consequence ordered the culture of silk at B. to be abandoned.
BESSAN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Herault, and cant. of Agde, on the river Herault, 21 m. NW of Agde, and 10 m. E of Beziers. Pop. 2,228.
BESSANS, a commune and village of Sardinia, 40 m. E of Saint-Jean-de-Maurienne, near the Arc, at an alt. of 4,583 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,000.
BESSARABA, a town of European Turkey, in Bulgaria, 6 m. S of Ruschuk, and 48 m. N of Ternova.

BESSARABIA, a district which, until the treaty of Bucharest, in 1812, formed a prov. of European Turkey, but now belongs to European Russia, situated between 45° 16' and 48° 39' N lat., and 26° 16' and 30° 23' E long. It is nearly of a peninsular form; and is bounded N and NE by the Dniester, which separates it from the gov. of Podolia and Kherson; on the E by the Black sea; on the S by the Danube, now its limit on the side of European Turkey; and on the W by the Pruth, which separates it from Turkish Moldavia. On the NW it borders on the Austrian empire, which it touches over a frontier line of 24 m. It extends 216 m. NNW and SSE; and is about 90 m. in its greatest breadth from E to W, gradually expanding as it approaches the sea. -A small branch of the Carpathian mountains covers its NW frontier; its southern part consists of a vast and continuous plain or steppe-the Bujak of the Tartars — which extends to the sea between the mouths of the Danube and the lower part of the Dniester, and, physically considered, forms the lower part of Moldavia.-Next to the Danube, the Pruth, and the Dniester, the principal streams in B. are the Jalpukh, the Kogalnik, the Sarata, the Riut, and the Botna. Almost all those rivers which flow to the Danube or the Black sea form limans, or broad shallow estuaries at their mouths. Amongst these are the gulf of the Dniester, and Lakes Murtaza, Sasik, Kotcheiol, Katlabuga, and Jalpukh.—The climate is mild, and the soil fertile. In some parts B. presents a thick bed of vegetable earth on a bottom of clay; in others, the soil is strongly impregnated with saltpetre. In the S it is composed of a mixture of sand and clay. The vegetable productions are millet, maize, oats, barley, melons, gourds, cucumbers, and asparagus of extraordinary size and thickness, peaches, and apricots: of the latter fruits, those of Ismael are the most renowned. Madder, saffron, hemp, and flax, BES grow spontaneously, and tobacco thrives. The N part of the prov. abounds in vines and wood; and the forests in this quarter yield excellent oak for shipbuilding, and are full of game. The quantity of wine produced in 1826 was 154,000 gallons. Storks and cranes seem indigenous in this prov., and the rivers swarm with fish. Cattle are extensively reared. There are quarries of marble, limestone, granite, and saltpetre; and numerous salt ponds and mineral springs.—The chief manufacturing industry exists in tan-works, iron-works, paper-mills, and cloth manufactures. The commerce consists in cloths, wines, beer, ewe-milk cheeses, furs, cattle, and salt; and is at present almost wholly in the hands of the Greeks. The customs amounted in 1839 to 319,134 rubles. - The inhabitants are chiefly Moldavians, and of the Greek faith; and are subject to the spiritual jurisdiction of the bishop of Kishenau. They are divided into two principal classes, - nobles and peasants. The first distinguish themselves in boyars, massils, and riseachs, that is to say, great proprietors, small proprietors, and half-peasants. The peasants are no longer serfs; but are only bound to give 12½ days' labour to their respective lords. The peasantry live on meal mixed with butter, fat, and milk, which is sometimes rendered more palatable by a few balls of boiled millet. Their bread is made of barley, and their drink is braga, a mixture of millet-meal and water which becomes acidulous by fermentation. In every cottage is a loom, on which the women weave linen, coloured stuffs for gowns, carpets, and a kind of net-work used for veils. They display great art and taste in these fabrics.—This prov. was but partly subdued by the Romans, who had only one colony at Cælia, now Kilia. Under the Turks, B. was a sanjak, and the governor, who resided at Bender, had an annual revenue of £3,000. The principal towns are Bender, Ismail, Akerman, Kilia, and Kischenef. The last of these is now the cap., though Kauscher was formerly the cap. of the part of Bessarabia which belonged to the khan of Tartary. B. was ceded to Russia—as we have said—by the peace of Bucharest, in 1812; and is now divided into 9 districts, the capitals of which, beginning from the S, are Ismail, Akerman, Kahul, Bender, Kichinev, Orgeev, Beltz, Soroka, and Khotin. From a Russian document recently published, it appears that B. contains a surface of 763 German, or 16,899 English sq. m.; and a pop. of 600,000. The same document estimates the pop. of B. in 1827 at 106,062 families who paid taxes, and 1,763 individuals without families. These numbers are vastly different from those given in the English edition of Malte Brun, and in the Parisian Dictionnaire Geographique. According to the latter authority, the pop. of B. was only 80,000; and of this number the nomade Tartars and Turks were estimated to form two-thirds. There are about 8,000 Gypsies in B.; and the immigrant colonists, chiefly Poles, Prussians, and Wurtembergers, may amount to 90,000. The majority of the pop. are of Moldavian descent. The Tartarian Nogays mostly withdrew from the country when it came under the dominion of Russia. The administration of this prov. has been assimilated under the present emperor to that of the other prov. of the Russian empire.

BESSASTADIR, or BESSESTAD, a town of Iceland, 7 m. SW of Reykiavik, at the extremity of the peninsula of Alptenäs. It was long the residence of the governors of the island. There is a gymnasium or high school here, with a library of about 1,000 vols. The rector has the title of Lector Theologiæ, with a salary of about £120, and there are two under-teachers, who have about £60 each. The Latin, Greek, Danish, and Icelandic languages are taught here; and some of the most learned works on Iceland and its literature have emanated from this establishment.
BESSAY-SUR-ALLIER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Neuilly-le-Real. Pop. 879.

BESSE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, arrond. of Issoire.—The cant. comprises 12 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 11,815.—The town is situated amid volcanic mountains, on one of the head-streams of the Creuse, at an alt. of 1,037 metres above sea-level, and 17 m. W of Issoire. It is built of basalt, and contains an hospital. Its commerce consists chiefly in cattle, lint, and cheese. In the environs are the cascades of Entraigues, the basaltic columns of the banks of the Malevoisière, the mineral waters of Condat, and Lake Pavin. Pop. 2,075.—Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Var, arrond. of Brignoles. The cant. comprises 6 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 8,525. The village lies near the Isolle, 9 m. SE of Brignoles, and possesses wine distilleries. Pop. 1,750.
BESSE-EN-OISANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isere, and cant. of Bourg-d'Oisans. Pop. 1,027.

BESSE'-SUR-BRAYE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, and cant. of St. Calais. The town stands on the r. bank of the Braye, 6 m. S of St. Calais; and contains manufactories of waxcandles, coarse cotton fabrics, and paper. Pop. 2,472.
BESSE'E (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, and com. of Largentiere. Pop. 100.
BESSEGE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Gard, and com. of Robiac. It contains blast furnaces; and coal is wrought in the environs. Pop. 218.
BESSELY GHAUT, or BISAVALI GHAT, a defile in the Western Ghauts, between the provinces of Mysore and Canara in Hindostan.
BESSELSLEIGH, a parish in Berkshire, 4 m. NW of Abingdon. Area 980 acres. Pop. 106.
BESSENAY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Rhone, and cant. of L'Arbresle, 15 m. W of Lyons. It possesses a considerable trade in wine. Pop. 1,958.
BESSESTAD. See BESSASTADIR.
BESSIERE-DE-L'AIR (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Cantal, and com. of Chaliers. Pop. 300.
BESSIERES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Montastruc. The town lies on the l. bank of the Tarn, 17 m. NE of Toulouse. Pop. 1,179.
BESSIN, a small district of France, in the ancient prov. of Normandy, of which Bayeux was the capital.

BESSINES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, arrond. of Bellac. The cant. comprises 7 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 10,406. The town is situated on the l. bank of the Gartempe, on the road from Paris to Toulouse, and 17 m. E of Bellac. It possesses an extensive trade in cattle, and a monthly fair. Pop. 1,179.
BESSINGBY, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. SW of Bridlington. Area 1,230 acres. Pop. 66.
BESSINGHAM, or Bassingham, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. SW of Cromer. Area 410 acres. Pop. 139.
BESSON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Allier and cant. of Souvigny, 8 m. SSW of Moulins. Pop. 1,370.

BESSUNGEN, or Bessingen, a village of Hesse- Damascus, to the NE of the Dead sea. It contains Darmstadt, about a mile S of Darmstadt, of which it forms a suburb. It contains cavalry-barracks, and fine gardens belonging to the duke. Pop. 1,848.
BESTHORPE, a parish in Norfolk, 1 m. E of Attleborough. Area 2,440 acres. Pop. 536.—Also a chapelry in the p. of South Searle, 7 m. NNE of Newark-upon-Trent. Area 510 acres. Pop. 327.
BESTIMESSARAS. See BETSIMACARAS. BET

BESUKI, a town of the island of Java, cap. of the prov. of the same name, situated near the NE coast, 60 m. SE of Surabaya. It contains a handsome palace, and possesses an active trade. The soil of the district is chiefly volcanic, and although imperfectly irrigated, is generally well-cultivated. Coffee was formerly its chief production. The pop. of the prov. is estimated at 184,620.
BESWICK, a township in the p. of Manchester, Lancashire. Area 60 acres. Pop. 345.-Also a chapelry in the p. of Kilnwick, E. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. N by W of Beverley. Area 1,430 acres. Pop. 211.
BESZTERCZE. See BISTRITZ.
BESZTERCZE-BANGA. See Neu-Sohl.
BET, a river of Marocco, in the district of Beni- Hassan, which runs WNW, and terminates in a swampy lake on the confines of Mamora forest.
BETAILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, and cant. of Vayrac. Pop. 1,643.
BETAINCOURT, a village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, and cant. of Donjeux, on the Rognon, 21 m. SE of Vassy. It contains some iron works. Pop. 307.

BETAISOR, or BATAISAR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Agra, on the r. bank of the Jumna. It is much resorted to by pilgrims. In the vicinity are the ruins of the ancient Hindu city of Sarapura.
BETANCURIA (SANTA MARIA DE), a town in the island of Fuerteventura, in the Canary group, on a small river about 5 m. from the W coast. It has considerable trade in barilla, cotton, and wine.

BETANIMENES, a district of the island of Madagascar, stretching along the E coast between the Betsiminsares territory on the N, and that of the Antaximes on the S. It is intersected by the Devurante, and is generally fertile. The inhabitants—sometimes called Siconas—are represented as agreeable in personal appearance, and pacific in disposition. The cap., Devurante, at the mouth of the river of the same name, is reputed to be the largest town in the island.

BETANZOS, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Galicia. It is situated on the declivity of a hill, the base of which is washed on the E by the Mandeo, and on the W by the Mendo, 4 m. S of the entrance of the former into Betanzos bay, and 12 m. SE of La Corunna. It is the cap. of a judicial partido, and was formerly a town of considerable importance. It contains some tanneries, and the environs afford good wine. Pop. 1,800.
BE'TAUCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Jussey. It has some blastfurnaces and forges. Pop. 539.
BETCHIK. See BESHEK.
BETCHWORTH, a parish in Surrey, 3½ m. W of Reigate. Area 3,660 acres. Pop. 1,140.
BETCOL. See BATTECOLLAH.
BET-CUM, a town of Nubia, in the district of Derkin, near the r. bank of the Mareb.
BETE, a town of New Granada, in the prov. of Choco, on the l. bank of the Atrato, 70 m. WSW of Antioquia, and 35 m. N of Citara.
BETELU, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Navarre, 17 m. NW of Pampeluna.
BETER RAS, a town of Syria, in the pash. of many architectural remains of its former importance. In the locality are numerous caverns, which are used as dwellings and storehouses by the inhabitants.
BETESKOI, a town of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, on the l. bank of the Irtish, 50 m. N of Omsk, and 290 m. SE of Tobolsk.
BETETE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of Chatelus. Pop. 1,003.

BETHANIA, or BETHANY, [Arab. EL AZIREZEH,] a small village of Syria, consisting of a few halfruined huts and an old mosque, 2 m. E of Jerusalem, on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives. It is inhabited for the most part by a colony of poor Arabs, who pasture a few sheep amongst its olive gardens, belonging to persons living in Jerusalem, whose trees and fruit also they guard. [Nugent.] In the vicinity is a grotto which is alleged to have been the tomb of Lazarus; and several other objects of legendary renown are shown to travellers here.
BETHANIA, a Moravian village of North Carolina, U. S., in Stokes co., 9 m. S of Germanton. Pop. 200.

BETHANY, a township in the state of Connecticut, U. S., in New Haven co., 46 m. SW of Hartford. Its eastern districts are mountainous, but the soil is generally good. Pop. in 1840, 1,170.—Also a township and village of New York, U.S., in Gennessee co., 252 m. W of Albany. The former presents an undulating surface, and is watered by the Little Tonawanda and Black creeks. The soil is a compound of sandy loam and clay. Pop. in 1840, 2,286. The vil. consists of about 35 houses, and contained, in 1840, 200 inhabitants.—Also a township of Pennsylvania, U. S., in Wayne co., 3 m. N of Honesdale, and 1 m. W of Dyberry creek. Pop. in 1840, 299.

BETHEL, a township in the state of Maine, U.S., in Oxford co., 18 m. NW of Paris. It lies on the Androscoggin river, and possesses a fertile soil. Pop. in 1840, 1,994.—Also a township of Vermont, in Windsor co., 34 m. SW of Montpelier. It is generally mountainous, but its soil is good and productive. It is watered by the White river and its branches. One of these affords excellent waterpower. Steatite or soap-stone abounds in the locality, and forms one of its most important articles of manufacture and export. Pop. in 1840, 1,886.— Also a village of Connecticut, in Fairfield co., 71 m. SW of Hartford. It contains about 60 houses, and has extensive manufactories of hats, combs, &c.- Also a township of New York, in Sullivan co., 60 m. W of Newburg. It is generally hilly, and is drained by branches of the Delaware. The soil is gravelly loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,483.—Also a township of Pennsylvania, in Berks co. Pop. in 1840, 1,458.— Also a township in Bedford co. It is mountainous, and is drained by Great Conoloway creek and its tributaries. The soil consists of calcareous loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,402.—Also a township in Delaware co. It presents a level surface, and is watered by Naaman's creek. The soil is clayey. Pop. in 1840, 386.—Also a township in Lebanon co. It is mountainous in the N, and is drained by Swatara creek and its tributaries. The soil is gravelly. Pop. in 1840, 1,662.—Also a township of Ohio, in Clark co., on Mad creek. Pop. in 1840, 2,033.—Also a township in Monroe co. Pop. in 1840, 545 .- Also a township in Miami co., on the E bank of the river of that name. Pop. in 1840, 1,574.—Also a township of Iowa, in Posey co. Pop. in 1840, 449.—Also a township of Michigan, in Branch co. Pop. in 1840, 335.—Also a township in St. Clair co. Pop. in 1840, 225. BETHELSDORF [Hung. BETLENFALVA], a village of Hungary, in the com. of Zips. Pop. 600.
BETHELSDORP, a town and missionary station of S Africa, in the prov. of Uitenhage, 8 m. NW of Port Elizabeth on Algoa bay, 450 m. E of Cape Town. In the vicinity is a saline lake, 3 m. in length.
BETHENCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Carnieres. It has manufactories of sugar from beet-root. Pop. 1,040.
BETHERSDEN, a parish in Kent, 6 m. WSW of Ashford. Area 6,410 acres. Pop. 1,011. BET
BETHESDA, a hamlet in the p. of Llanllochid, in Carnarvonshire, on the Ogwen.
BETHINES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of St. Savin. Pop. 1,246.
BETHISY - SAINT - PIERRE, or BETHISY-LABUTTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Cressy. Pop. 1,561.

BETHLEHEM, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Nievre, and com. of Clamecy, of which it forms a suburb. It became in 1180 the residence of the bishop of Bethlehem, on his expulsion by the Saracens, and, in consequence, the seat of the small bishopric of that name. Pop. 1,600.

BETHLEHEM, or BEIT-EL-LEHM [i. e. 'the House of Bread'], a village of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, 6 m. S of Jerusalem, on the road to Hebron, occupying the site of the ancient town of that name, the birth-place of David, and of the Messiah. It is a large village, and has "a massive and imposing appearance at a little distance" [Wilson], being finely situated on the brow of an eminence sloping on both the N and S sides, and commanding an extensive view of the surrounding country. The streets are more regular and clean than those of most of the towns of Palestine. [Nugent.] The soil of the surrounding territory is fertile, and clothed with vineyards, almond-groves, and fig orchards. The wine of B. is of excellent quality, and resembles Marsala. [Wilde.] The inhabitants amount to about 2,000, chiefly Catholics and Greeks, with a few Armenians. No Mahommedan or Jew resides here. The men are a peculiarly fine race; and the women are regarded as the handsomest in Syria. They support themselves by agriculture, and the manufacture of various figures and trinkets for pilgrims, such as representations of the holy sepulchre, crucifixes, rosaries, and relics, which are formed in mother-of-pearl from the Red sea, or of olive-wood, agate, jasper, or bituminous limestone. The ecclesiastical edifices of B. have been described by many travellers. They consist of a Latin Franciscan convent; a Greek convent; the church of the Nativity, originally built by the empress Helena, the mother of Constantine, "a place of considerable grandeur," and the chancel of which is immediately over the Cave of the Nativity, as it is called. This sanctum sanctorum of B. is a low rocky cavern, with an arched roof. Its floor is paved with white marble, and its marble walls are hung with silken draperies; and the whole is illuminated by 32 lamps. It is 12 paces in length, by 4 in breadth. [Olin.] There is but one small mosque in B.—From B. to Jerusalem the road leads over a country so much less rugged than the usual pilgrim-roads of Palestine and Syria, that a pedestrian can easily accomplish it in an hour and a half .- Clarke .- Richardson .- Robinson.—Nugent.—Olin.— Wilson.

BETHLEHEM, a township in Grafton co., in the state of New Hampshire, U. S., 88 m. NW of Concord. Pop. 779.—Also a township in Hunterdon co., New Jersey, 40 m. N by W of Trenton. Pop. 2,370. -Also a township in Albany co., New York, 5 m. S of Albany. Pop. 3,238.—Also a town in Northampton co., in Pennsylvania, 48 m. W of N of Philadelphia, on the N bank of the Lehigh. Pop. 2,989. There is a celebrated Moravian school here.—Also a township in Stark co., in Ohio, on the Tuscarawas. Pop. 2,019.—Also a township in Coshocton co., Ohio. Pop. 827.—Also a village in Clark co., in Iowa, on the W bank of the Ohio.—Also a village in Oglethorpe co., Georgia, 65 m. NNE of Milledgeville.
BETHLEM, a township in Litchfield co., in the state of Connecticut, U.S., 43 m. WSW of Hartford, on the Pomperang, a branch of the Housatonic. Pop. 776.
